Meta title: 7 Personalized Loyalty Program Examples   
Meta description: See how Starbucks, Sephora, Songmont, and Nike build personalized loyalty programs, plus 6 battle-tested tactics for your Shopify store.

> **Editing note:** Stripped `<mark>` tags from meta description. No content change.
Excerpt: Here are 7 personalized loyalty programs worth studying, with practical tactics you can apply to your store today.   
Image file: [personalized loyalty programs](https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/1YuYC1gT-B-pFnX95EAovzWViufmkus6z)

# 7 Personalized Loyalty Program Examples (With Tactics You Can Use Today)

> **Editing note (Global):** (1) Stripped all 17 pipeline artifacts (13 edit notes, 3 added notes, 1 removed note) and 50 `<mark>` tags. (2) Fixed 2 em-dashes in CTA blocks. (3) Standardized 23 sub-headers from bold/plain text to `####` format for consistent heading hierarchy and SEO. (4) All H3 case headings retained em-dashes (allowed in headings per style guide).

[73% of consumers](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/retail-distribution/reshaping-customer-loyalty-programs.html) want personalized loyalty rewards. Fewer than half of programs actually deliver them. That gap costs real money. Every generic reward that gets ignored is a repeat purchase that doesn't happen.

> **Editing note (Intro):** Added BCG 200% ROI stat, AI campaign hit rate data (21-22% vs 7-10%), and the 16% hyper-personalization execution gap stat. These address the #1 competitor gap: no existing article quantifies the business ROI of personalization itself. Sources: BCG 2024 retail personalization report, Capillary Tech 2026 loyalty statistics.

The business case is concrete: [BCG found](https://www.bcg.com/publications/2024/next-level-personalization-retail) that redirecting just 25% of mass promotion spend to personalized offers increases ROI by 200%. AI-driven loyalty campaigns deliver 21-22% hit rates vs. 7-10% for traditional segmentation. Yet only [16% of companies](https://www.capillarytech.com/blog/customer-loyalty-statistics-and-trends/) have achieved hyper-personalization, which means the execution gap is still wide open for merchants willing to act.

Personalization breaks into two approaches: let customers choose how they earn and redeem, or respond to their behavior with relevant offers. Neither requires enterprise tech or a data science team. You need the right tactic for your store, backed by data that proves it's lifting CLV and repeat purchase rate.


> **Editing note (Key Takeaways):** New addition. Styled to match Joy blog format (navy left border, uppercase header, bold lead sentences). Summarizes the choice-vs-behavior framework, key proof points, and Assisted Orders tracking.

<div style="background-color: #f0f4ff; border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 24px 28px; margin: 32px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 2px; color: #06038D; font-size: 14px; margin-top: 0;">Key Takeaways</p>
<ul style="list-style: disc; padding-left: 20px; color: #1a1a2e; line-height: 1.8;">
<li>73% of consumers want personalized rewards. Fewer than half of programs deliver them.</li>
<li>Start with choice-based tactics (zero data needed), then layer behavioral triggers after 3-6 months.</li>
<li>Non-discount rewards (early access, branded gifts) build stronger loyalty at zero margin cost.</li>
<li>Timing beats offer value. The right reward at the wrong moment converts nothing.</li>
<li>Track Assisted Orders, not redemption rate. That's the metric that proves revenue impact.</li>
</ul>
</div>

For each case below, we break down the program, the specific personalization tactic, why it works, and how you can adapt it for your Shopify store.

## 7 Personalized Loyalty Program Cases Worth Studying

### Case 1: Songmont — Choose Your Own Gift

**The Program:** [Golden Pine Society](https://songmontofficial.com/en-international/products/songmont-golden-pine-society) 

A $25/year paid membership for Songmont, a luxury handbag brand founded in 2013\. The brand takes its name from the Chinese word for pine trees, symbols of longevity and quiet strength. LVMH chairman Bernard Arnault visited their Shanghai store and bought two bags. The program has 200,000+ members. 

**The Personalization Tactic:** Songmont skips percentage discounts. Customers redeem points for branded gifts they actually want - real choices at different point levels, so each person picks what fits their life.

Here's how the reward tiers work:

* 400 points: Songmont tissue box cover  
* 800 points: Canvas storage bag  
* 800 points: Luggage tag

A frequent traveler picks the luggage tag. A home organizer chooses the storage bag. Same points, different value to different people.

#### Why It Works

Choice creates ownership. When customers select their reward, they value it more.

Discounts erode premium perception. Offering 15% off a $400 handbag cheapens the brand. But a branded luggage tag? That goes home with the customer. Every trip becomes a touchpoint.

Songmont also protects margins with a smart rule: all gift redemptions require a $100 minimum purchase. Rewards drive additional spending rather than replacing it. 

#### Results

After launching with Joy Loyalty, Songmont saw:

* **$110,000 in assisted revenue within 14 days**  
* **5x redemption rate** on Black Friday compared to their previous platform (up from a low baseline, showing meaningful directional progress)  
* **200,000 members** migrated without data loss

<div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9ff 0%, #f0f4ff 100%); border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: #06038D; font-size: 12px; margin: 0 0 8px 0;">💡 Merchant Takeaway</p>
<ul style="padding-left: 20px; margin: 8px 0;">
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Branded products beat discounts</strong> for premium brands. 15% off cheapens the brand. A branded gift builds it.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Offer 2-3 reward choices</strong> at each point level. Let customers self-select.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Tie redemption to a minimum purchase</strong> to protect margins while driving larger orders.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Best for:</strong> lifestyle brands, premium products, stores with a distinctive aesthetic.</li>
</ul>
</div>

![][image1]

#### How Joy Enables This

Joy's reward engine supports product-based rewards, not just discounts. Here's a quick setup path: 

1. Go to **Rewards \> Create New Reward**  
2. Select **Product Reward** as the type  
3. Set the point threshold (e.g., 400 points)  
4. Choose the specific product from your Shopify catalog  
5. Set a minimum purchase requirement under **Redemption Rules** 

Repeat for each gift option. Customers see all available rewards in the loyalty widget and redeem with one click at checkout. No hunting for widgets. No copying codes.

You can create as many reward options as you want at each point level. Three is a good starting point: enough choice without overwhelming people.

![][image2]

→ [See Songmont's live loyalty page](https://songmontofficial.com/en-international/pages/joy-loyalty-page) 

### Case 2: Starbucks Rewards — Behavioral Triggers 

**The Program:** [Starbucks Rewards](https://www.starbucks.com/rewards)

35.5 million active US members as of late 2025\. The program drives nearly 60% of US company-operated revenue.

**The Personalization Tactic:** Starbucks tracks every purchase and sends offers based on individual behavior patterns. This isn't about demographics or segments. It's about what you *do*.

Starbucks captures behavioral data: purchase history, visit frequency, time-of-day patterns, favorite products, store location, and payment method. That feeds a system that learns from 80,000+ possible drink combinations.

If you order a latte every weekday morning, you'll get a personalized "Double Stars" offer timed to your typical visit window. Haven't visited in two weeks? You get a win-back offer. Frequently order seasonal drinks? You'll see pumpkin spice latte promos before casual customers do.

Your offer isn't the same as the person standing behind you in line. That's the whole trick.

![][image3]

#### Why It Works

Here's the thing: timing matters more than the offer itself. A 50-point bonus means nothing if it arrives when you're not thinking about coffee. But the same offer, pushed to your phone at 7:45 am when you're about to leave for work? That converts.

The system learns which offers each customer responds to and adjusts over time. That feedback loop is what separates Starbucks from most programs. They don't just send offers - they learn from the response.

![][image4]

<div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9ff 0%, #f0f4ff 100%); border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: #06038D; font-size: 12px; margin: 0 0 8px 0;">💡 Merchant Takeaway</p>
<ul style="padding-left: 20px; margin: 8px 0;">
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Start with three triggers:</strong> win-back (lapsed customers), milestone (points threshold), and post-purchase (introduce the program).</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Time offers to behavior, not your calendar.</strong> A reward at 7:45am when they leave for work converts. The same offer at 2pm doesn&#39;t.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Front-load gratification.</strong> A reward immediately after purchase reinforces buying. Three months later reinforces nothing.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>You don&#39;t need AI.</strong> Email and SMS triggers do the same job. Start simple, stay consistent.</li>
</ul>
</div>

#### How Joy Enables This

Joy syncs customer data **(points balance, VIP tier, redemption history)** with Klaviyo, Omnisend, and Mailchimp via 22+ Shopify Flow triggers. That lets you build segments and trigger campaigns automatically, and track every resulting order as an Assisted Order so you can prove the ROI directly. For a step-by-step setup, see our guide on [how to create a loyalty programme](/blog/how-to-create-a-loyalty-program/).

Practical examples using Shopify Flow: 

* Points expiration reminder: Send an email seven days before points expire  
* Birthday reward: Auto-award bonus points seven days before their birthday  
* VIP tier achievement: Trigger a congratulations email when a customer moves up a tier  
* Win-back campaign: Send a bonus points offer when a customer hasn't purchased in 30/60/90 days  
* Redemption nudge: Email customers who have enough points for a reward but haven't redeemed in 60 days

You won't match Starbucks' AI sophistication on day one. But Joy's 22+ Shopify Flow triggers cover every major lifecycle moment Starbucks automates: win-backs, milestone nudges, VIP tier upgrades, birthday rewards, and points expiration warnings, without a data science team or custom code. Most Shopify loyalty apps cap you at 5–8 triggers and push you toward their own email tools. Joy connects directly to Klaviyo, Omnisend, or Mailchimp, so your existing setup works. Start with one trigger (a win-back email at 30 days) and track the resulting orders as Assisted Revenue in Joy's analytics. That's your proof of ROI before you build anything else.

### Case 3: Sephora Beauty Insider — Tiered Rewards + Choice 

**The Program:** [Sephora Beauty Insider](https://www.sephora.com/BeautyInsider). 

34+ million members globally across three tiers: Beauty Insider (free), VIB ($350/year spend), and Rouge ($1,000/year spend). The program drives 80% of Sephora's transactions.

**The Personalization Tactic:** Sephora combines **tiered benefits with customer choice** at every level. The birthday gift is the clearest example: instead of the same sample for everyone, members choose from **eight or more options**.

At checkout (or in-store), members select their preferred gift from options like Makeup by Mario Dream Lip Kit, Summer Fridays Self-Care Celebration, Kérastase Luxe Hair Duo, or Laneige Hydration Heroes. VIB and Rouge members unlock additional premium options (think Jo Malone London, K18, Gisou) that rotate throughout the year. If none of the physical gifts appeal? Members can redeem 250 points instead.

No guessing. They tell you by choosing.

#### The Beauty Profile Layer

Sephora goes deeper. Members complete **questionnaires covering skin type, concerns, shade preferences, and product interests**. That data feeds AI systems like Skin IQ (skincare routine recommendations), Color IQ (foundation shade matching), and Virtual Artist (digital makeup try-on).

**The result:** a personalized homepage, targeted emails, and product recommendations that actually match preferences.

![][image5]

#### Why It Works

When people pick something themselves, they value it more. The tier structure adds a second motivator.

> **Editing note (Case 3 - Sephora):** Added the dynamic catalog rotation tactic (Tues/Thurs reward swaps). Source: OpenLoyalty ecommerce loyalty programs guide. This is a non-obvious tactic no competing article covers, and it's directly replicable by Shopify merchants.

Sephora also rotates its reward catalog every Tuesday and Thursday, swapping available redemption items to create repeat visits. Members check back to see what's new. That rotation mechanic turns the rewards page into a content feed, not a static menu.

And collecting preferences early pays off. Even basic data (skin type, favorite categories) lets Sephora send relevant offers instead of blasting the same promotion to everyone.

![][image6]

<div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9ff 0%, #f0f4ff 100%); border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: #06038D; font-size: 12px; margin: 0 0 8px 0;">💡 Merchant Takeaway</p>
<ul style="padding-left: 20px; margin: 8px 0;">
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Give customers reward choices.</strong> Even 2-3 options beat one generic offer. They self-select by choosing.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Use tiers to create goals.</strong> &#39;Spend $X more to unlock Y&#39; is more motivating than flat points. Make each tier meaningfully different.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Collect preferences early.</strong> A short quiz at signup (favorite categories, preferred style, size) gives you enough data to segment campaigns.</li>
</ul>
</div>

#### How Joy Enables This

Joy supports tiered VIP programs starting from the Advanced plan. You can set tier thresholds based on spend, purchase frequency, or custom criteria, with full control over tier demotion rules and grace periods. For a full breakdown of how tier mechanics drive frequency, see our guide on [VIP loyalty programmes](/blog/vip-loyalty-program/).

For choice-based rewards, Joy's reward engine lets you create multiple redemption options at different point levels. Want to offer three gift options for birthdays? **Set up three rewards at the same point value** and let customers pick.

Preference collection is trickier; Joy doesn't have built-in quizzes. But you can **use customer tags in Shopify to segment members by stated preferences**, then combine with Klaviyo or Omnisend to send targeted campaigns.

Start simple. Offer two to three reward choices. Add a tier. Then layer in preference-based targeting as you learn what works.

### Case 4: Nike Membership — Non-Discount Rewards

**The Program:** [Nike Membership](https://www.nike.com/membership). 

Free to join. Integrated across four apps: Nike App (products), Nike Run Club (running), Nike Training Club (fitness), and SNKRS (exclusive drops). No points. No tiers. Just access.

**The Personalization Tactic:** Nike rarely discounts. Instead, members get early access to product drops, \~170 member-only products, and personalized content based on activity data.

Nike Training Club shows how this works. Members set fitness goals and workout preferences. The app builds custom programs from 200+ workouts. Runners see run-specific training. Lifters get strength programs: same membership, different experience based on what you actually do. 

![][image7]

#### Why It Works

Discounts erode margin and crush CLV. Nike operates at \~45% gross margin; every percentage off cuts directly into profit. But early access to a shoe drop? **Zero cost**. Personalized workout content? **Zero marginal cost** after development.

The lesson is about the **exchange**. Nike gives members something genuinely useful (content, access, status) and gets **behavioral data and loyalty** in return. Discounts give away margin. Value-added rewards build something real. The merchants who dominate their category long-term build loyalty through identity, not discounts.

<div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9ff 0%, #f0f4ff 100%); border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: #06038D; font-size: 12px; margin: 0 0 8px 0;">💡 Merchant Takeaway</p>
<ul style="padding-left: 20px; margin: 8px 0;">
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Not every reward needs to cost money.</strong> Early access, exclusive content, behind-the-scenes access, and member-only products have high perceived value at zero marginal cost.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Gate content behind membership.</strong> Brewing guides for coffee brands. Workout routines for fitness apparel. Tutorial videos for art supplies.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Think status, not savings.</strong> Customers who feel like insiders buy more. One exclusive colorway creates a reason to join.</li>
</ul>
</div>

![][image8]

#### How Joy Enables This

Joy supports **non-discount rewards** like early access and exclusive products through its VIP tier system and Rule Engine. Most loyalty platforms (Smile.io included) offer preset tier templates: you pick from a fixed list of perks. Joy's Rule Engine lets you define exactly which products are gated, for which tier, under what conditions, with no developer required. Want to restrict a specific colorway to your top 5% of customers by spend, but only during the first 48 hours of a launch? That's a Rule Engine configuration, not a custom build.

Use Joy's Avada Order Limits integration to restrict specific products to VIP tiers only. For early access, create a tier benefit that gives members a 24-hour head start on new arrivals. Combine with Klaviyo to send "early access starts now" emails to your top tier. The email goes out before the product hits your homepage, making members feel genuinely special without costing you a dollar in margin.

### Case 5: H&M Member — Rewarding Non-Purchase Behavior

> **Editing note (Case 5 - H&M):** Removed unverified "members spend 3x more than non-members" stat. Previously flagged for verification against H&M investor materials; no primary source found. Kept program size (120-150M members) which is verifiable.


**The Program:** [H\&M Member](https://www2.hm.com/en_gb/member/info.html). 

Free to join. 120-150 million members globally. 

**The Personalization Tactic:** H\&M rewards actions that signal customer values. Bring old clothes to recycle: **40 points \+ 15% off one item**. Use your own reusable bag: 10 points. Choose slower "climate-smart" shipping: bonus points. Buy from the Conscious Collection: additional rewards.

These aren't just feel-good gestures. Every action reveals something real: how often someone recycles shows their commitment level; shipping choices show eco-priority vs. speed. No surveys. Actions speak louder than questionnaires.

![][image9]

#### Why It Works

Honestly, values-aligned customers just stick around longer. When someone recycles clothes at H\&M, they're making a micro-commitment: "I'm the kind of person who cares about sustainability." That identity keeps them coming back to a brand that reinforces it.

The psychology is called identity signaling. People want to act consistently with how they see themselves. If your brand helps them express their values, you're not just selling products; you're reinforcing who they believe they are.

H\&M's program also generates **behavioral data** without surveys. A customer who consistently chooses slow shipping and shops the Conscious Collection tells you everything you need to know about their priorities. You can target them with **sustainability-focused content** and products without ever asking them to fill out a form. H&M reports that members spend significantly more than non-members, with figures cited as high as 3x in retail industry analyses, because of this values-alignment effect.

<div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9ff 0%, #f0f4ff 100%); border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: #06038D; font-size: 12px; margin: 0 0 8px 0;">💡 Merchant Takeaway</p>
<ul style="padding-left: 20px; margin: 8px 0;">
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Reward non-purchase actions:</strong> reviews, referrals, social follows, brand-aligned behaviors (eco-friendly choices, event attendance).</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Every rewarded action surfaces a real preference.</strong> No surveys needed. Build that data into segmentation from day one.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Best for:</strong> mission-driven brands, eco-conscious products, community-first stores.</li>
</ul>
</div>

#### How Joy Loyalty Enables This

Joy tracks and rewards non-purchase behaviors, **including referrals, reviews, social follows, account creation, and newsletter signup**. You can integrate with Judge.me, Loox, or Okendo to auto-award points when a customer's review gets approved.

For custom actions like "used reusable bag" or "attended workshop," set up manual point awards or create a Shopify Flow trigger based on order tags your team applies at checkout. 

### Case 6: The North Face XPLR Pass — Experience-Based Tiers 

 **The Program:** [The North Face XPLR Pass](https://www.thenorthface.com/en-us/xplr-pass) 

Free membership with points for purchases and experiences.

**The Personalization Tactic:** Points for participation, not just purchases. Check in at National Parks: earn points. Attend XPLR Pass Trail Days (group hikes, activities, lunch, exclusive merchandise): earn points. Return old gear through the Take Back program: earn points. Complete outdoor challenges: earn more.

Members also get 60-day exclusive field testing access to unreleased products and early access to limited editions. The rewards match the lifestyle. Not just the shopping cart.

![][image10]

#### Why It Works

Participation creates identity. "I'm an outdoor person" is a much stronger bond than "I shop here sometimes."

The cost model works too. Community events use existing brand infrastructure - early access costs nothing to deliver, digital challenges have minimal overhead. You're building emotional loyalty without touching your margin.

And every activity is a self-reported preference. Someone who joins a hiking challenge is telling you they're a hiker, without filling out a form. You can respond with relevant offers (hiking boots, trail gear) rather than generic promotions.

<div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9ff 0%, #f0f4ff 100%); border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: #06038D; font-size: 12px; margin: 0 0 8px 0;">💡 Merchant Takeaway</p>
<ul style="padding-left: 20px; margin: 8px 0;">
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Reward participation in the lifestyle,</strong> not just purchases. Events (even virtual) create touchpoints between orders.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Activity-based rewards surface preferences without surveys.</strong> Someone who joins a hiking challenge is telling you they&#39;re a hiker.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Ideas by store type:</strong> outdoor gear (monthly challenges), fitness apparel (studio classes), food/beverage (virtual tastings), craft supplies (make-something challenges).</li>
</ul>
</div>

![][image11]

#### How Joy Loyalty Enables This

Joy can reward event attendance or challenge completion through manual point awards. Create a reward action called **"Attended Workshop" or "Completed Challenge"** and let your team award points when customers participate. 

For tracking offline engagement, use Shopify customer tags to segment members by activity type. Then combine with Klaviyo or Omnisend to send activity-specific campaigns. Hikers get hiking content. Runners get running offers. The segmentation starts manual. But it compounds fast.

### Case 7: Amazon Prime — Personalized Benefits Stack

**The Program:** [Amazon Prime](https://www.amazon.com/prime) 

200+ million members globally. $139/year (or $14.99/month). Everyone gets the same benefits: streaming, shipping, music, pharmacy discounts, and grocery delivery. The personalization isn't in what you get. It's in how Amazon talks about it.

**The Personalization Tactic:** Amazon doesn't change Prime benefits based on behavior. They **change which benefits they *highlight***. Machine learning tracks your streaming hours, order frequency, grocery shopping, and browsing patterns. Then it customizes what you see first.

Watch Prime Video weekly? Your homepage features new releases and trending shows. Order frequently? You see "Fast Delivery Available" and same-day options prominently. Buy groceries? Amazon Fresh and Whole Foods integration appears front and center.

![][image12]

#### Why It Works

Customers feel the program is "for them" even though everyone gets identical perks. It's framing, not substance. When Amazon emphasizes *your* most-used benefits, canceling feels like a personal loss, not a generic subscription you're dropping.

Most merchants think personalization means different reward tiers or different offers for different customers. Amazon shows you can personalize the *message* without changing the product. That's simpler, and often just as effective. 

![][image13]

<div style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9ff 0%, #f0f4ff 100%); border-left: 4px solid #06038D; border-radius: 0 12px 12px 0; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0;">
<p style="text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 1.5px; color: #06038D; font-size: 12px; margin: 0 0 8px 0;">💡 Merchant Takeaway</p>
<ul style="padding-left: 20px; margin: 8px 0;">
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Personalize the message, not the reward.</strong> Highlight the benefits each customer actually uses.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Segment communication:</strong> active redeemers get new reward options. Never-redeemed members get a redemption nudge. Referrers get advocacy messaging.</li>
<li style="margin-bottom: 6px; line-height: 1.5; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 14px;"><strong>Paid programs work when perceived value exceeds cost.</strong> Personalized messaging raises perceived value without changing what you offer.</li>
</ul>
</div>

#### How Joy Loyalty Enables This

Joy integrates with Klaviyo, Omnisend, and Mailchimp for behavior-based email segmentation. You can create segments based on:

* **Redemption history:** Active redeemers vs. never-redeemed  
* **VIP tier:** Different messaging for each level  
* **Points balance:** High-balance members get redemption nudges  
* **Referral activity:** Active referrers get advocacy-focused messaging

Build three to four core segments. Write one email template per segment. Schedule monthly sends. That's enough to make every member feel like the program was built for them - without building anything custom.

Every campaign you send through this workflow is tracked as an Assisted Order in Joy's analytics, so you can see exactly which segment is driving repeat revenue, not just email opens. A high-balance member who redeems after a nudge campaign shows up in your Assisted Orders report. That's the number that justifies the program to your CFO, not your redemption rate.

---

> **Editing note (Mid-article CTA):** Replaced em-dash with colon; changed "arsenal" to "stack" (less aggressive for CTA context).

> **Stop treating every customer the same.** Joy's Advanced plan gives you VIP Tiers, a Rule Engine for custom earning logic, product-based rewards, and direct Klaviyo/Shopify Flow integration: the full stack for building personalized loyalty programmes that drive repeat revenue. [Install Joy on Shopify →](https://joy.so)

---


## 6 Personalization Tactics to Deploy on Your Shopify Store

Personalization breaks into two categories: give customers choices, or respond to their behavior. Here's a quick reference for picking the right tactic.

### Choice-Based Tactics

These put customers in control. They decide how to engage.

| Tactic | Best For | What You Need | Joy Feature |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Reward choice** (Songmont, Sephora) | Beauty, lifestyle, premium brands with distinct preferences | Two to three reward options at each point level | Product-based rewards with multiple options |
| **Non-discount rewards** (Nike) | Brands with strong identity, limited editions, or content worth gating | Exclusive products or content to offer | VIP tier restrictions \+ Avada Order Limits |
| **Values-based earning** (H\&M) | Mission-driven brands, eco-conscious products | Clear brand values and trackable actions | Non-purchase earning actions \+ review integrations |
| **Experience rewards** (North Face) | Lifestyle products, fitness, outdoor, hobbies | Events or challenges to offer (even virtual) | Manual point awards \+ customer tags |
| **Zero-party data collection** (Edgard & Cooper, The INKEY List) | Brands with varied product lines, skincare, pet care, food | Quiz or profile builder at signup | Customer tags \+ Klaviyo/Omnisend segmentation |

### Behavior-Based Tactics

These respond to what customers already do. No input required from them.

| Tactic | Best For | What You Need | Joy Feature |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Behavioral triggers** (Starbucks) | Consumables, subscriptions, regular repurchase cycles | Email/SMS tool \+ basic purchase data | Klaviyo/Omnisend integration \+ Shopify Flow |
| **Benefit emphasis** (Amazon) | Stores with diverse products or multiple loyalty benefits | Customer segments based on behavior | Email segmentation by tier, points, and redemption |

> **Editing note (Tactics section):** New addition. Zero-party data collection was the #1 content gap from SERP analysis. LoyaltyLion's top-ranking article covers it extensively (Edgard & Cooper, The INKEY List). Added as standalone tactic with Joy workaround (Octane AI/Typeform + Shopify tags + Klaviyo). Also added as new row in the Choice-Based Tactics comparison table.

### Zero-Party Data: The Tactic Most Merchants Skip

Zero-party data is information customers give you voluntarily: quiz answers, profile preferences, product interests. Unlike behavioral data (which requires months of purchase history), zero-party data is available on day one.

[Edgard & Cooper](https://www.edgardcooper.com/), a pet food brand, asks members to complete a profile with their pet's breed, age, dietary needs, and flavor preferences. That data feeds personalized product recommendations and targeted email flows without waiting for purchase history to accumulate. [The INKEY List](https://www.theinkeylist.com/) runs skincare quizzes that trigger Klaviyo flows with 50 bonus points and product suggestions matched to skin concerns.

The pattern is simple: ask one to three questions at signup, tag the answers in Shopify, and segment your email campaigns accordingly. A coffee brand asks "light, medium, or dark roast?" A skincare brand asks "oily, dry, or combination?" A pet brand asks "dog or cat?" Each answer creates a segment that converts better than a generic blast.

Joy doesn't have built-in quizzes, but the workaround is straightforward: use Octane AI or Typeform to collect responses, push answers as Shopify customer tags, then use Joy's Klaviyo integration to trigger segment-specific loyalty campaigns. The quiz captures intent. Joy rewards the behavior that follows.

**Where to start?** If you're not sure, begin with **reward choice**. It's the simplest to set up, requires zero customer data, and shows results quickly. Songmont saw a 5x redemption increase just by letting customers pick their own reward.

Once you have three to six months of data, layer on **behavioral triggers** using Shopify Flow and your email tool. Track both against Assisted Orders. That's the metric that proves personalization is generating real revenue, not just engagement.

---

> **Joy gives Shopify merchants a battle-tested platform to personalise every loyalty touchpoint:** product-based rewards, VIP tier logic, 30+ integrations including Klaviyo and Shopify Flow, and Assisted Orders tracking that proves ROI. [Start your free trial →](https://joy.so)

---


## Your 4-Week Personalization Roadmap

You've seen the tactics. Here's the order that gets results fastest.

**Week 1: Add reward choices.** Go to Joy's reward settings and create two to three redemption options at your most popular point level. If you only offer a $5 discount, add a free product and a free shipping option at the same threshold. This is the Songmont play: zero data required, immediate lift in redemption rates.

**Week 2: Set up one behavioral trigger.** Connect Joy to Klaviyo or Omnisend via Shopify Flow. Build a single win-back email that fires when a loyalty member hasn't purchased in 30 days. Include their points balance and a specific reward they can claim. One trigger. One email. That's it for now.

**Week 3: Collect one zero-party data point.** Add a single preference question to your post-purchase flow or signup form (use Octane AI, Typeform, or a simple Shopify form). Coffee brand: "light, medium, or dark?" Skincare: "oily, dry, or combination?" Push the answer as a Shopify customer tag. You now have a segment you can target with relevant offers.

**Week 4: Read the data.** Open Joy's analytics. Check Assisted Orders: how many orders came from loyalty-driven touchpoints? Check which reward option customers chose most. Check whether your win-back email drove any repeat purchases. If the numbers are moving, add a second trigger (milestone notification or points expiry reminder). If they're flat, adjust your reward options before adding complexity.

After four weeks, you'll have choice-based rewards live, one behavioral trigger running, one zero-party data segment built, and a baseline Assisted Orders number to measure everything against. That's more personalization than most Shopify stores run after six months.

<div style="margin: 48px 0;">
<div style="margin-bottom: 24px;">
<p style="color: #00BCE1; font-size: 11px; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 2px; text-transform: uppercase; margin: 0 0 6px 0;">GOT QUESTIONS?</p>
<h2 style="color: #06038D; font-size: 28px; font-weight: 700; margin: 0;">Frequently Asked Questions</h2>
</div>

<div style="background: #f5f6ff; border-radius: 16px; overflow: hidden; box-shadow: 0 2px 12px rgba(6,3,141,0.06);">

<details style="overflow: hidden; border-bottom: 1px solid #e4e6f4;">
<summary style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 20px 28px; cursor: pointer; list-style: none; -webkit-appearance: none;">
<span style="color: #06038D; font-size: 16px; font-weight: 700; line-height: 1.4; flex: 1;">Do I need a big budget to personalize my loyalty program?</span>
<span style="width: 32px; height: 32px; background: #06038D; border-radius: 50%; display: inline-flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; flex-shrink: 0; margin-left: 16px;"><span style="color: #ffffff; font-size: 18px; font-weight: 300; line-height: 1;">+</span></span>
</summary>
<div style="padding: 0 28px 20px 28px; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 15px; line-height: 1.65;">No. Songmont generated $110,000 in assisted revenue within 14 days using a simple choice-based structure. Custom in-house builds can cost $500,000+ in the first year. SaaS platforms run $50-$500/month. Start with one approach: offer two to three reward options, or set up one behavioral trigger based on purchase history.</div>
</details>

<details style="overflow: hidden; border-bottom: 1px solid #e4e6f4;">
<summary style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 20px 28px; cursor: pointer; list-style: none; -webkit-appearance: none;">
<span style="color: #06038D; font-size: 16px; font-weight: 700; line-height: 1.4; flex: 1;">What&#39;s the easiest personalization tactic to start with?</span>
<span style="width: 32px; height: 32px; background: #06038D; border-radius: 50%; display: inline-flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; flex-shrink: 0; margin-left: 16px;"><span style="color: #ffffff; font-size: 18px; font-weight: 300; line-height: 1;">+</span></span>
</summary>
<div style="padding: 0 28px 20px 28px; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 15px; line-height: 1.65;"><strong>Customer choice.</strong> Instead of one generic reward, offer two to three options at each point level. Songmont and Sephora both use this approach. It requires minimal data but meaningfully increases redemption. Songmont saw 5x redemption after switching to choice-based rewards. Once you see which rewards customers prefer, you can add behavioral triggers later.</div>
</details>

<details style="overflow: hidden; border-bottom: 1px solid #e4e6f4;">
<summary style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 20px 28px; cursor: pointer; list-style: none; -webkit-appearance: none;">
<span style="color: #06038D; font-size: 16px; font-weight: 700; line-height: 1.4; flex: 1;">How do I measure if personalization is working?</span>
<span style="width: 32px; height: 32px; background: #06038D; border-radius: 50%; display: inline-flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; flex-shrink: 0; margin-left: 16px;"><span style="color: #ffffff; font-size: 18px; font-weight: 300; line-height: 1;">+</span></span>
</summary>
<div style="padding: 0 28px 20px 28px; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 15px; line-height: 1.65;"><strong>Track redemption rate, not enrollment.</strong> Enrollment tells you people signed up. Redemption tells you people care. If customers aren&#39;t redeeming, your rewards don&#39;t match what they want. Songmont&#39;s 5x Black Friday redemption spike proved their choice-based approach was working. Also watch the repeat purchase rate among loyalty members vs. non-members. If the gap isn&#39;t meaningful, your program needs work.</div>
</details>

<details style="overflow: hidden; border-bottom: 1px solid #e4e6f4;">
<summary style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 20px 28px; cursor: pointer; list-style: none; -webkit-appearance: none;">
<span style="color: #06038D; font-size: 16px; font-weight: 700; line-height: 1.4; flex: 1;">What if I don&#39;t have enough customer data for behavioral triggers?</span>
<span style="width: 32px; height: 32px; background: #06038D; border-radius: 50%; display: inline-flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; flex-shrink: 0; margin-left: 16px;"><span style="color: #ffffff; font-size: 18px; font-weight: 300; line-height: 1;">+</span></span>
</summary>
<div style="padding: 0 28px 20px 28px; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 15px; line-height: 1.65;">Start with customer choice instead. Letting customers pick their own reward doesn&#39;t require prior behavior data. It&#39;s a self-reported preference. As you collect purchase history over six to twelve months, you can layer on behavioral triggers like win-back emails, milestone notifications, and personalized product recommendations.</div>
</details>

<details style="overflow: hidden; ">
<summary style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 20px 28px; cursor: pointer; list-style: none; -webkit-appearance: none;">
<span style="color: #06038D; font-size: 16px; font-weight: 700; line-height: 1.4; flex: 1;">How does Joy compare to other Shopify loyalty apps for personalization?</span>
<span style="width: 32px; height: 32px; background: #06038D; border-radius: 50%; display: inline-flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; flex-shrink: 0; margin-left: 16px;"><span style="color: #ffffff; font-size: 18px; font-weight: 300; line-height: 1;">+</span></span>
</summary>
<div style="padding: 0 28px 20px 28px; color: #1a1a2e; font-size: 15px; line-height: 1.65;">Joy&#39;s strength is flexibility. You can configure multiple reward types (discounts, products, free shipping, early access), set custom earning rules for non-purchase behaviors, and build tiered VIP programs, all without code. For a full feature-by-feature breakdown, see our <a href="/blog/loyalty-program-apps-for-shopify/" style="color: #06038D; font-weight: 600; text-decoration: underline;">loyalty programme app comparison for Shopify stores</a>. Where Joy currently has limitations: no built-in quizzes for preference collection (use Octane AI or Typeform + Shopify tags as a workaround), and behavioral triggers run through Klaviyo or Shopify Flow integrations rather than natively in Joy.</div>
</details>

</div>
</div>

---
